data.title: SA analyst upgrades/downgrades: MU, COST, GOOG, AMZN
data.description:
Recent analyst actions from Seeking Alpha feature notable upgrades for Micron Technology (MU) and Costco Wholesale Corporation (COST), driven by record earnings and macroeconomic tailwinds respectively. On the downside, Alphabet (GOOG) faces a downgrade amid concerns over massive capital expenditures and margin compression, while Amazon (AMZN) draws caution due to significant valuation premiums despite strong fundamentals..

Upgrades

  • Micron Technology (MU): Upgrade to Buy by Louis Gerard. The analyst points to record-breaking Q3 results and Strategic Customer Agreements that lock in significant capacity, providing downside protection in future downcycles.

    "The reason for this staggering quarter is not simply a better cycle but rather the scale of Strategic Customer Agreements, as Micron disclosed 16 legally binding take-or-pay contracts running from 2026 through 2030. All of these will lock in roughly 20% of DRAM and 33% of NAND capacity, carrying around $100B of remaining performance obligations that are backstopped by about $22B of customer collateral, of which around $18B is cash already on deposit."

  • Costco Wholesale (COST): Upgrade to Short-term Buy by APAC Research. The analyst cites high fuel prices and inflation as key drivers pushing consumers toward value-seeking behavior, benefiting Costco's membership model.

    "Inflation outside of food has also accelerated. This could be a boon for Costco as more and more customers turn to the company to save money. Once members are locked in with a membership, many will likely stay even after fuel costs come down."

Downgrades

  • Alphabet (GOOG): Downgrade to Hold by Esxeleryn Analytics. The analyst warns that Alphabet's aggressive AI infrastructure spending will trigger near-term margin compression and a significant depreciation burden.

    "On the downside, the main factor is the CapEx/depreciation gravity well and drop in asset turnover. To add more here, Alphabet’s infrastructure buildout is forming an adverse state for its forward income statements...If macroeconomic conditions tighten, Alphabet will be trapped with a huge fixed-cost depreciation drag that may lead to large GAAP margin compression."

  • Amazon.com (AMZN): Valuation Concerns Raised by Hunting Alphas. While fundamentally bullish on AWS and e-commerce growth, the analyst flags a steep valuation premium that creates notable overvaluation risk.

    "The key driver of this impressive growth is quite simply massive enterprise demand for AI infrastructure and cloud computing...Besides providing a revenue boost, Amazon's custom chips, such as Trainium, are also expected to result in capex and opex cost savings."

data.title: Nissan looks to cut costs on Mexico-built models as U.S. tariffs weigh
data.description:
Nissan Motor (NSANY) is working to reduce costs on vehicles built in Mexico to offset the impact of U.S. tariffs that have made some models harder to sell in the United States, Bloomberg News reported on Wednesday, citing an interview with Chief Executive Ivan Espinosa.

Espinosa said 25% tariffs on vehicles imported from Mexico were making part of Nissan's lineup more difficult to sell in the U.S. market, where affordability remains under pressure.

The Japanese automaker is seeking to make its Mexico-built vehicles, including the Sentra sedan and Kicks crossover, more competitive while retaining production in Mexico to benefit from lower labor costs.

Nissan (NSANY) has previously said tariffs on the Sentra and Kicks add about $2,500 to $3,000 to the cost of each vehicle.

Espinosa also said Nissan (NSANY) would continue investing in U.S. manufacturing, adding that producing vehicles locally remained a sensible strategy despite the weaker yen. The company produced about 60% of the vehicles it sold in the United States locally last year, up from roughly 45% at the beginning of 2025.

Shares of the company were up about 3% in the afternoon trading.

data.title: BTIG’s top small and mid-cap stocks for H2, ranked by SA Quant
data.description:
BTIG analysts selected their favorite stock picks over a 12-month investment period within the small- and mid-cap space for the second half of the year.

These are BTIG’s highest conviction investment ideas with smaller caps that are buy-rated by its research team.

“As we look ahead to 2H’26, we see evidence that the broadening can continue,” analyst said. “Financials (XLF) remain strong across banks (KBE), REITs (XLRE), and insurance (KIE), (IAK). Biotech (XBI), (BBH), (IBB) is coming on strong as IBB breaks out of a five-year base. We have seen Mag7 (MAGS) quickly roll over, and while they can probably stabilize, we continue to think the semi/AI trade is vulnerable to a more meaningful unwind lower.”

data.title: Fedspeak hits most hawkish level since late 2023
data.description:
The tone of Federal Reserve officials has shifted significantly in recent weeks, with Bloomberg Economics’ proprietary Fedspeak model having reached its most hawkish level since the end of 2023.

The index, which tracks and quantifies the underlying sentiment of speeches and statements made by Fed policymakers, recently surged to 5.83 by the end of June 2026, according to a chart circulated by Charles Schwab's Kevin Gordon.

This steep upward trajectory marks a stark reversal from the deeply dovish territory the index occupied through much of 2024 and early 2025. 

As such, the data indicate that central bank officials are increasingly signaling a preference for restrictive monetary policy. For example, Cleveland Fed President Beth Hammack said earlier in the week that higher interest rates may be needed to bring inflation to the monetary authority's 2% target. But she's not necessarily supporting a rate hike in the July meeting.

Markets, too, are leaning more hawkish, with traders on prediction marketplace Kalshi seeing a greater than 50% chance the Fed will increase rates this year.

Meanwhile, Fed Chair Kevin Warsh said Wednesday that expectations of inflation over the last four weeks have come down, and inflation risks have come down. He added that anyone who expected the Fed to be content with inflation above its target "will be disappointed."

data.title: Senator Warren calls for stronger crypto law after Trump's latest financial disclosure
data.description:
Senator Elizabeth Warren has called for stronger crypto legislation to prevent U.S. President Donald Trump and his family from profiting off the digital asset.

"The crypto legislation heading to the Senate floor must stop the President and his family from continuing to profit off crypto," said Warren in a social media post.

Trump's latest financial disclosure showed he earned more than $1.4B from his family's cryptocurrency ventures in 2025, including nearly $800M from World Liberty Financial, the crypto company he co-founded with his sons.

That income, which the president splits with family members, included more than $520M from sales of crypto tokens and over $250M from the sale of interests in the World Liberty business.

Crypto is by far the largest driver of income for Trump.

"We are seeing the greatest onslaught of corruption in the history of mankind in the past 18 months," said former Trump White House lawyer. Ty Cobb, told CNN.

The House passed the Digital Asset Market Clarity Act bill in July 2025 and the Senate Banking Committee in May 2026. The landmark crypto legislation seeks to create a clear regulatory framework for digital assets.

The bill is heading to the Senate floor.

Dear Readers: We recognize that politics often intersect with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

Bitcoin ETFs: (IBIT), (ARKB), (GBTC), (BRRR), (BTCO), (HODL), (BTCW), (FBTC), (BITB), and (EZBC).

data.title: Dow downgraded at RBC on weak demand, outsized capex commitment
data.description:
Dow Inc. (DOW) wobbles between small gains and losses Wednesday as RBC Capital downgraded the chemical company to Sector Perform from Outperform with a $28 price target, slashed from $51, saying underlying polyethylene supply and demand remains unchanged, underpinning price weakness.

Despite a price uplift in 2026, Dow's (DOW) fundamental earnings backdrop remains unchanged compared to year-end 2025, as no structural supply/demand changes have materialized since the start of the U.S.-Iran conflict, RBC analyst Arun Viswanathan said.

While initially expecting the steepening cost curve, feedstock disruptions, and dislocations in demand to result in some accelerated asset shutdowns in Europe and Asia, the analyst notes that major announcements of closures appear to have slowed, driven by an uptick in Chinese exports and CTO production; as such, risk has skewed to the downside on a reopening of the Strait of Hormuz, and earnings could step down to 2024 levels in 2027.

Viswanathan also said Dow's (DOW) Alberta cracker project could be a major draw on cash over the next several years—~$7.5B of gross capital spending commitment, net $6B including ~$1.5B of government incentives—with the project planned for construction through 2029, while acknowledging the potential for improved earnings power and for the company to JV the project.

Dow (DOW) shares had declined for 11 consecutive trading days before gaining $0.06 on Tuesday.

data.title: U.S. won't renew USMCA, US trade rep says - report
data.description:
The United States decided against renewing its trade deal with Mexico and Canada, U.S. Trade Representative Jamieson Greer said, according to a media report on Wednesday. That will trigger annual reviews of the agreement for the next decade, introducing more uncertainty for North American manufacturers.

The U.S.-Mexico-Canada Agreement, also called USMCA, won't expire for another 10 years, assuming no country decides to exit it. Yearly reviews will take place instead of a longer-term renewal, potentially leading to years of heated negotiations over the rules addressing supply chains and low tariff levels for farmers, automakers, and energy companies.

Ahead of the official announcement, Grier told Bloomberg News that the administration is "not prepared to rubber stamp the agreement." 

"We think there are substantial issues," he said, noting that several changes are needed to remedy imbalances.

The first Trump administration negotiated the USMCA in 2020. At the time, President Donald Trump called it the "best and most important trade deal ever made." But in his second term, the president was frustrated that he wasn't allowed to raise tariffs to address trade deficits with Canada and Mexico.

Under USMCA's terms, the administration could have extended the agreement by 16 more years on its sixth anniversary, July 1, 2026.

The US Dollar Index (DXY) rose 0.1% in Wednesday midday trading. The 10-year Treasury yield slipped less than a basis point to 4.463%.

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: Palo Alto Networks sees bullish views at BTIG
data.description:
BTIG increased Palo Alto Networks' (PANW) price target to $380 from $333 while reiterating the stock as its top pick with a Buy rating.

Shares of the cybersecurity solutions provider rose about 4% on Wednesday.

"Our independent fieldwork on PANW the last six months has been consistently improving. And our early work into CQ’26 earnings points towards continued momentum. At a high level, PANW’s breadth across network security, cloud, endpoint, SIEM [Security Information and Event Management], observability, and identity gives it the broadest platform in cybersecurity. And our field checks indicate that platform initiatives are translating into larger deal sizes and stronger cross-sell opportunities," said analysts led by Gray Powell.

The analysts think this is showing up in results as the company is dominating its core network security target market and is substantially outgrowing peers in the Secure Access Service Edge, or SASE, space. In addition, the analysts said that Palo Alto is aggressively making inroads into the $8.6B SIEM market with XSIAM and having better than expected early success with Chronosphere. Both of these products help the company better unify its product strategy and build a data moat, according to the company.

"Lastly, we see sizable expansion opportunities in identity with CyberArk and have been picking up positive early data points on Prisma AIRS/AI Security. All in, the momentum within PANW’s installed base and expansion into higher growth markets gives us greater confidence in the company’s ability to sustain mid-teens growth over the next few years," said Powell and his team.

data.title: iPhone purchase intent increases in US, Europe but not due to Apple Intelligence: survey
data.description:
Consumers in Europe and the U.S. demonstrated an increased intent to purchase an Apple (AAPL) iPhone at some point in the next 12 months, according to a survey by UBS.

However, Apple Intelligence, which many financial analysts believed would drive up demand, is not playing a role in the increased interest.

Apple shares edged up 2% by noon trading on Wednesday.

"12-month global iPhone purchase intent in the US rose approximately 300 bps YoY to about 20%, as China fell 100 bps to 15%," said UBS analysts, led by David Vogt, in an investor report. 

The U.K. increased by 600 bps to 18%, Germany rose by 400 bps to 14%, and Japan inched up 100 bps to 13%. 

"Apple Intelligence is not driving upgrade activity as total respondents indicated that they would upgrade sooner for Apple Intelligence features declined to 24%, while those noting no impact on their purchasing decision rose to 31%," Vogt added.

The survey also found that the average age of iPhones in the user base declined to 22.9 months, but that remained near the survey's all-time high of 24.1 months. The all-time high occurred during the second quarter of 2025.

What's more, Apple is expected to reveal a new foldable phone during its annual September launch event. While the survey did not indicate significant interest in foldable smartphones in general, it did reveal meaningful consumer interest in an Apple foldable smartphone.

"Yes, but we believe a foldable iPhone will be more material in driving consumer demand than Apple's recently announced AI features," Vogt noted. "We estimate iPhone sell-in of 261.6M units (+15.7% YoY) in FY26 due to stronger-than-expected demand for the iPhone 17 and a degree of pull-in from anticipated price increases. While we estimate that incremental foldable units could be as high as 5M units (~2% upside to UBSe), our forecasts do not factor in potential demand destruction from expected iPhone price increases. As overall interest in upgrading for Apple Intelligence was relatively muted, we believe that a foldable iPhone would be a more compelling reason for consumers to upgrade, considering its favorability spread over a foldable smartphone."
